text: The right hemicolectomy, according to the technique we have performed, provides the positioning of three doors.
The surgeon extends the vasilio-psychocolic tent and proceeds to identify the duodenal window, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon opens the duodenal window using traction and counter-traction maneuvers with two tweezers, progressing towards the genital opening.
The surgeon highlights the upper belly of the mesenteric vessels, specifically focusing on the upper mesenteric vein, using precise dissection techniques.

The surgeon performs an extended DII lymphadenectomy, carefully dissecting lymphatic tissue around the right colon sheath to ensure accurate disease staging.
The surgeon identifies the origin of the hypochloric vessels, including arteries and veins, and proceeds with clamping and separating these structures, followed by the exhalation of the cells.
The surgeon highlights and releases the second and initial third portions of the duodenum, subsequently exposing the body and head of the pancreas.

The surgeon maintains the integrity of the fasciae of Toldt and Gerota while applying constant traction and release on the body and head of the pancreas. This maneuver highlights the colic vessels on the right, including arteries and veins, which are then released, ligated, and sectioned.
The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy of the right colon, ensuring thorough removal of lymphatic tissue for disease staging.

The surgeon continues the dissection, highlighting a branch of the trunk of L, which is visibly veined, and proceeds to tie and section it.
A gauze is placed within the dissection area on a hemostatic scope to aid in performing a correct dissection, serving as a reference point during the procedure.

The surgeon isolates and sections the right branches of the medial colic artery within the transverse mesocolon, linking and separating them until the marginal branches of the medial colic are exposed.

After completing the dissociation, the surgeon highlights the right colon wall and proceeds to section the omentum, exporting it in block with the operating piece. The surgeon then detaches the epiploic colon.
The surgeon performs epiproic colon detachment, facilitating lymphadenectomy of two lymph nodes, with visible separation of the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the lower margin of the right gastroepiploic vessels, preparing for further dissection or ligation.

The video shows the previously performed section of a branch of the colic artery of the gastroepiploic trunk. The surgeon proceeds with the sectioning of the coloepiploic region, lowering and dissociating the right hepatic flexure.
The surgeon maintains the margin of the peritoneum while dissecting and separating it from the Morrison's pouch.
The surgeon performs an incision on the right parietocolic ligament following the dissociation of the epiproic colon.
The surgeon incises the right parietocolic ligament, facilitating the section of the transverse colon using a surgical stapler with a blue cartridge, typically 3.5 mm in size.
The surgeon engraves the root of the mesentery and continues the parietocolic dissociation on the right side, fully opening the root of the mesocolon.

The surgeon mobilizes the mesoileum and proceeds to section it using either a combined ultrasound device or separate ligation of the artery and vein, visually isolating these structures for precise division.
The surgeon measures approximately ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve, likely preparing for a subsequent surgical step in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the no-touch dissection technique for the right hemicolectomy and proceeds to package the anastomosis, ensuring the anatomical structures are properly aligned and secured.
The surgeon performs a lateral-lateral isoperistaltic anastomosis using a linear suture, creating a connection with a diameter of approximately 4.5 centimeters between the bowel segments.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by closing the eye of the suture, finalizing the connection between the intestinal segments.
Normally the eye is closed through a chiseled or continuous point with monofilament autostatic thread at slow absorption.
The surgeon carefully checks and performs closure at the mesenteric margin of the anastomosis, ensuring secure linkage to prevent potential complications.
The surgeon completes the daivieni suture and proceeds to close the mesocolon and mesoideal breach, ensuring the prevention of potential internal hernias.
The surgeon completes the procedure by extracting the resected colon segment through an incision made using the Fannesty technique.
